title: Fiction Rule of Thumb
alt: Except for anything by Lewis Carroll or Tolkien, you get five made-up words per story.  I'm looking at you, Anathem.
transcript:
[Line graph shown with an inverse curve.]
[Y-Axis: Probability book is good.]
[X-Axis: Number of words made up by author.]
[The curve becomes less steep as the number of words increases.]
"The Elders, or Fra'as, guarded the farmlings (children) with their krytoses, which are like swords but awesomer..."
explanation:
Randall uses a graph that purports that the more words an author makes up, the less likely their book is any good. To demonstrate this, he provides an example where a hypothetical author uses three made-up words in a single sentence: "Fra'as", "Farmlings" and "Krytoses". The author clearly does not see that having to insert explanations of all the made-up words makes the sentence extremely clumsy. In addition, the sentence uses the word "awesomer" (like the other words that were given explanations, also given in italics), which is nonstandard English meaning "more awesome". This could be seen as a fourth made-up word, although it would be unlikely to have been created by the hypothetical author of the sentence and might instead just be read as a more standard emphasis. It is also possible that this word was just included in order to make the sentence appear less formal. 

The title text declares that the average author is allowed five invented words per book before this rule is invoked against them, but mentions that  and  are exceptions, as they are both very famous, well-respected writers  .

Randall also makes a dig at , a speculative fiction novel by  about a monastic order on another planet that studies science, mathematics, and philosophy. The book is noteworthy for having a very large number of  enough to require its own glossary. One of the more common other-worldly words is fraa (without an apostrophe), as an analogue to ""/"".
